w:r.w:t: NFS Exporting Parent ZFS Dataset Instead of User Subdatasets

All user data written inside containers — installed applications, conda environments, files — was being stored in the parent ZFS dataset (datapool/users) instead of each user's individual subdataset (datapool/users/testuser2, datapool/users/testuser3). This meant per-user quotas were not being enforced, user data was not isolated, and ZFS showed incorrect usage per user.
Each user limited to their quota
All users writing to same parent dataset

NFS was exporting the parent dataset /datapool/users instead of each user's individual subdataset. When the container mounted /mnt/nfs/users/testuser3 and wrote files, those writes went into the parent dataset's filesystem layer — bypassing the testuser3 ZFS subdataset entirely. ZFS subdatasets only receive data when they are the direct NFS export target.

ZFS datasets behave like separate filesystems stacked on top of each other. When you create datapool/users/testuser3, ZFS mounts it at /datapool/users/testuser3 — effectively hiding the parent's view of that directory. However, NFS does not automatically follow this stacking. If NFS exports the parent (/datapool/users), clients see a flat directory view and writes go into the parent's storage layer, not the child datasets.
# What the container mount looked like:
datapool/users (parent dataset) ← all writes landed here
Run these to confirm the issue:
# Check ZFS dataset usage — if parent shows large REFER
# and subdatasets show 24K, data is in the wrong place
# Check what NFS is actually exporting
# Verify testuser3 quota — if it shows 'none' or full pool size
# BAD: quota = none (means no quota set or not applied)
# BAD: shows 34G total (full pool) instead of user quota
Step 1 — Fix /etc/exports to Export Subdatasets Individually
Replace the parent export with individual exports for each user subdataset:
# Remove old parent export and replace with per-user exports
# Must show individual subdataset paths, NOT /datapool/users
Step 2 — Create Per-User NFS Mount Points
Step 3 — Unmount Old NFS, Apply New Exports, Remount
Step 4 — Update fstab for Persistence Across Reboots
Step 5 — Clean Old Data from Parent Dataset
Data that was incorrectly written to the parent dataset must be removed. This requires temporarily unmounting the subdatasets to expose what's underneath:
# Unmount subdatasets to expose parent data
# Delete old data from parent directories

Cleaning old data from the parent dataset will delete any previously installed applications and user files. This is unavoidable if data landed in the wrong place. Going forward, all new installs will correctly land in the subdataset.
# 1. ZFS usage should show near-zero in parent
# 4. Install something inside container, then check ZFS usage grows
# USED should now show ~500MB — confirms data going to correct dataset
# 5. df inside container shows correct quota
# Must show: 25G total (or 15G for testuser2) — NOT 34G (full pool)
When df -h /home/ubuntu shows the user's quota (not the full pool size) and sudo zfs list shows USED growing in the subdataset — the fix is working correctly.
How to Avoid This in Future
Always export subdatasets in NFS, never the parent
/etc/exports must list each user's subdataset path individually
Always mount subdatasets directly in fstab
Each user gets their own mount point — never mount the parent and use subdirectories
Verify after setup with a write test
Write a test file inside container, check sudo zfs list shows USED growing in the subdataset
Set quotas before first container launch
If quota is not set, user can fill the entire pool
Create ZFS dataset, set quota, export in NFS, add fstab entry, create mount point — all four steps required
This is the correct relationship between ZFS, NFS, and containers:
LaaS NFS/ZFS Issue Fix Guide v1.0 — March 17, 2026 — End of Document
